randomNumber:
; NASM syntax (Linux 64-bit example - requires linking C library)
section .data
    msg db "Random number (simple LCG, seeded by time): %d", 10, 0
    fmt db "%d", 0 ; Format for printf

section .bss
    seed resd 1 ; Reserve 4 bytes for seed (LCG uses 32-bit)

section .text
    extern printf
    extern time   ; From C library
    global main  ; Use main for easier linking with libc

main:
    push rbp
    mov rbp, rsp
    sub rsp, 8 ; Align stack

    ; --- Seed the LCG using time(NULL) ---
    mov rdi, 0      ; Argument NULL for time()
    call time       ; rax = current time (seconds since epoch)
    mov [seed], eax ; Store lower 32 bits as seed

    ; --- Simple LCG: seed = (a * seed + c) % m ---
    ; Using parameters from POSIX standard (a = 1103515245, c = 12345)
    ; Modulo 2^31 is implicit due to 32-bit overflow
    mov eax, [seed]    ; Load current seed
    mov ebx, 1103515245 ; Multiplier 'a'
    mul ebx            ; edx:eax = eax * ebx (unsigned multiplication)
    add eax, 12345     ; Add constant 'c'
    mov [seed], eax    ; Store the new seed

    ; --- Get a value in a range [1, 100] ---
    ; Use the upper bits of the result for better randomness (often recommended)
    ; Shift right to get rid of lower bits (e.g., shr eax, 16) - omitted for simplicity
    ; Here, just use the lower bits directly (less random quality)
    mov edx, 0         ; Clear edx for division
    mov ecx, 100       ; Divisor (for range 0-99)
    div ecx            ; eax = quotient, edx = remainder (our random number 0-99)
    add edx, 1         ; Shift range to 1-100

    ; --- Print the number using printf ---
    mov edi, msg       ; Format string
    mov esi, edx       ; The random number (passed as int)
    mov eax, 0         ; No floating-point arguments in vector registers
    call printf

    ; --- Exit program ---
    mov rsp, rbp
    pop rbp
    mov eax, 0         ; Return 0 from main (standard C convention)
    ret

; To assemble and link (requires GCC/Clang):
; nasm -f elf64 snippet.asm -o snippet.o
; gcc snippet.o -o snippet -no-pie
; ./snippet
reverseString:
; NASM syntax (Linux 64-bit example - uses C library puts)
section .data
    myString db "Hello World", 0 ; Null-terminated string
    len equ $ - myString - 1     ; Calculate length at compile time
    msg_orig db "Original: ", 0
    msg_rev db "Reversed: ", 0
    newline db 10, 0             ; Newline character

section .text
    extern puts ; Using puts from C library for simplicity
    global main

main:
    push rbp
    mov rbp, rsp
    sub rsp, 8 ; Align stack

    ; --- Print original string ---
    mov rdi, msg_orig
    call puts
    mov rdi, myString
    call puts
    mov rdi, newline
    call puts

    ; --- Reverse the string in-place ---
    mov rsi, myString      ; Pointer to start (left)
    mov rdi, myString      ; Pointer to end (right)
    add rdi, len           ; Move to the null terminator
    dec rdi                ; Move to the last character ('d')

reverse_loop:
    cmp rsi, rdi           ; Compare left and right pointers
    jge reverse_done       ; If left >= right, we are done

    mov al, [rsi]          ; Load char from left
    mov bl, [rdi]          ; Load char from right

    mov [rsi], bl          ; Store right char at left position
    mov [rdi], al          ; Store left char at right position

    inc rsi                ; Move left pointer right
    dec rdi                ; Move right pointer left
    jmp reverse_loop

reverse_done:
    ; --- Print reversed string ---
    mov rdi, msg_rev
    call puts
    mov rdi, myString
    call puts
    mov rdi, newline
    call puts

    ; --- Exit program ---
    mov rsp, rbp
    pop rbp
    mov eax, 0      ; Return 0 from main
    ret

; To assemble and link (requires GCC/Clang):
; nasm -f elf64 snippet.asm -o snippet.o
; gcc snippet.o -o snippet -no-pie
; ./snippet
fibonacci:
; NASM syntax (Linux 64-bit, prints first 10 terms using printf)
section .data
    msg db "Fibonacci Sequence (first 10 terms):", 10, 0
    fmt_num db "%lld ", 0 ; Format for printing long long (64-bit)
    newline db 10, 0

section .text
    extern printf
    global main

main:
    push rbp
    mov rbp, rsp
    sub rsp, 8 ; Align stack

    mov rdi, msg
    mov eax, 0 ; No vector args for printf
    call printf

    mov rcx, 10 ; Number of terms to generate (n)
    mov r8, 0   ; t1 = 0 (current term)
    mov r9, 1   ; t2 = 1 (next term)
    mov r10, 0  ; Loop counter (i)

fib_loop:
    cmp r10, rcx
    jge fib_done ; If i >= n, exit loop

    ; --- Print current term (r8) ---
    mov rdi, fmt_num
    mov rsi, r8  ; The number to print (passed as long long)
    mov eax, 0
    call printf

    ; --- Calculate next term: nextTerm = t1 + t2 ---
    mov r11, r8  ; temp = t1
    add r11, r9  ; temp = t1 + t2 (this is nextTerm)

    ; --- Update t1 and t2 for next iteration ---
    mov r8, r9   ; t1 = t2
    mov r9, r11  ; t2 = nextTerm

    inc r10      ; i++
    jmp fib_loop

fib_done:
    ; --- Print a final newline ---
    mov rdi, newline
    mov eax, 0
    call printf

    ; --- Exit program ---
    mov rsp, rbp
    pop rbp
    mov eax, 0      ; Return 0 from main
    ret

; Note: Uses printf for simplicity. Pure assembly output would
; involve converting numbers to strings and using sys_write.
; Uses 64-bit registers (r8, r9, r11) to handle larger Fibonacci numbers.

; To assemble and link (requires GCC/Clang):
; nasm -f elf64 snippet.asm -o snippet.o
; gcc snippet.o -o snippet -no-pie
; ./snippet
factorial:
; NASM syntax (Linux 64-bit, calculates 5! using printf)
section .data
    num dq 5        ; Input number (use qword for larger factorials)
    msg db "Factorial of %lld = %lld", 10, 0
    fmt_ll db "%lld", 0 ; Format specifier for long long (64-bit)

section .text
    extern printf
    global main

main:
    push rbp
    mov rbp, rsp
    sub rsp, 8 ; Align stack

    mov rbx, [num]  ; Number n
    mov rax, 1      ; Initialize result (factorial) to 1
    mov rcx, rbx    ; Use rcx as loop counter, starting from n

    ; Handle base case: 0! = 1 (already covered by initialization if n=0)
    ; Handle negative input? (Skipped here, assumes non-negative)

fact_loop:
    cmp rcx, 1      ; Loop while rcx > 1
    jle fact_done   ; If rcx <= 1, calculation is done

    mul rcx         ; rax = rax * rcx (unsigned 64-bit multiply)
                    ; Result is in rax. rdx will contain upper 64 bits if overflow occurs.
                    ; Simple version ignores overflow check.
    dec rcx         ; Decrement counter
    jmp fact_loop

fact_done:
    ; --- Print the result using printf ---
    mov rdi, msg      ; Format string
    mov rsi, [num]    ; First argument: the original number n
    mov rdx, rax      ; Second argument: the calculated factorial
    mov eax, 0        ; No vector registers used for printf
    call printf

    ; --- Exit program ---
    mov rsp, rbp
    pop rbp
    mov eax, 0      ; Return 0 from main
    ret

; Note: Uses printf. Pure assembly requires number-to-string conversion.
; Uses 64-bit registers. A robust version would check RDX after MUL for overflow.

; To assemble and link (requires GCC/Clang):
; nasm -f elf64 snippet.asm -o snippet.o
; gcc snippet.o -o snippet -no-pie
; ./snippet
primeCheck:
; NASM syntax (Linux 64-bit, checks if 29 is prime)
; Requires linking C library for printf and math library for sqrt (-lm)
section .data
    num dq 29         ; Number to check (64-bit)
    msg_prime db "%lld is a prime number.", 10, 0
    msg_not_prime db "%lld is not a prime number.", 10, 0

section .text
    extern printf
    extern sqrt ; From libm (math library)
    global main

main:
    push rbp
    mov rbp, rsp
    sub rsp, 16 ; Align stack for function calls (16-byte boundary needed by some libc functions)

    mov rax, [num] ; Load the number n into rax

    ; --- Handle base cases ---
    cmp rax, 1
    jle not_prime    ; n <= 1 is not prime

    cmp rax, 3
    jle is_prime     ; n = 2 or n = 3 is prime

    ; --- Check divisibility by 2 ---
    test rax, 1      ; Check if the lowest bit is 0 (even)
    jz not_prime     ; If even (and > 2), not prime

    ; --- Check divisibility by 3 ---
    mov rdx, 0       ; Clear rdx for division
    mov rcx, 3
    mov r11, rax     ; Save n before division modifies rax
    div rcx          ; rax = n / 3, rdx = n % 3
    mov rax, r11     ; Restore n
    cmp rdx, 0       ; Is remainder zero?
    je not_prime     ; If divisible by 3 (and > 3), not prime

    ; --- Calculate sqrt(n) to limit the check range ---
    cvtsi2sd xmm0, rax ; Convert rax (integer n) to double precision float in xmm0
    call sqrt          ; xmm0 = sqrt(xmm0) (calls C library function)
    cvtsd2si rbx, xmm0 ; Convert result back to 64-bit integer in rbx (this is the limit)

    ; --- Check factors from 5 up to sqrt(n), stepping by 6 ---
    ; We check i and i+2 where i starts at 5 and increments by 6 (5, 11, 17, ...)
    mov rcx, 5      ; Start checking divisor i = 5

prime_loop:
    cmp rcx, rbx    ; Compare divisor i with limit sqrt(n)
    jg is_prime     ; If i > sqrt(n), no factors found, it's prime

    ; Check n % i
    mov rdx, 0
    mov r11, rax     ; Save n
    div rcx          ; rax = n / i, rdx = n % i
    mov rax, r11     ; Restore n
    cmp rdx, 0
    je not_prime     ; If divisible by i, not prime

    ; Check n % (i + 2)
    add rcx, 2       ; Calculate i + 2
    cmp rcx, rbx    ; Compare i+2 with limit sqrt(n) - optimization
    jg is_prime     ; If i+2 > sqrt(n), we can stop early

    mov rdx, 0
    mov r11, rax     ; Save n
    div rcx          ; rax = n / (i+2), rdx = n % (i+2)
    mov rax, r11     ; Restore n
    cmp rdx, 0
    je not_prime     ; If divisible by i+2, not prime

    ; Increment i by 4 to get to the next i in the 6k+/-1 pattern (i was i, then i+2, next is i+6)
    add rcx, 4
    jmp prime_loop


is_prime:
    mov rdi, msg_prime
    mov rsi, [num]
    mov eax, 0 ; No vector args for printf
    call printf
    jmp exit_program

not_prime:
    mov rdi, msg_not_prime
    mov rsi, [num]
    mov eax, 0
    call printf
    jmp exit_program


exit_program:
    mov rsp, rbp
    pop rbp
    mov eax, 0      ; Return 0 from main
    ret

; To assemble and link (requires GCC/Clang and math library):
; nasm -f elf64 snippet.asm -o snippet.o
; gcc snippet.o -o snippet -no-pie -lm
; ./snippet
readFile:
; NASM syntax (Linux 64-bit using syscalls)
section .data
    filename db "example.txt", 0 ; File to read (MUST EXIST!)
    errmsg_open db "Error opening file", 10
    len_errmsg_open equ $ - errmsg_open
    errmsg_read db "Error reading file", 10
    len_errmsg_read equ $ - errmsg_read
    buffer_size equ 256          ; Size of buffer to read into

section .bss
    buffer resb buffer_size      ; Reserve buffer space
    fd resq 1                    ; Reserve space for file descriptor (64-bit)

section .text
    global _start

_start:
    ; --- Open file (sys_open) ---
    mov rax, 2         ; syscall number for sys_open
    mov rdi, filename  ; const char *pathname
    mov rsi, 0         ; flags (O_RDONLY = 0)
    mov rdx, 0         ; mode (not needed for O_RDONLY)
    syscall            ; rax = file descriptor or negative error code

    cmp rax, 0         ; Check if rax is negative (error)
    jl open_error      ; Jump if less than 0 (error)

    mov [fd], rax      ; Store the file descriptor

    ; --- Read loop (sys_read) ---
read_loop:
    mov rax, 0         ; syscall number for sys_read
    mov rdi, [fd]      ; int fd
    mov rsi, buffer    ; void *buf
    mov rdx, buffer_size ; size_t count
    syscall            ; rax = bytes read, 0 for EOF, or negative error code

    cmp rax, 0         ; Check for error or EOF
    jle read_done      ; If rax <= 0, jump to handle EOF or error

    ; --- Write buffer contents to stdout (sys_write) ---
    mov rdx, rax       ; Number of bytes actually read becomes count for write
    mov rax, 1         ; syscall number for sys_write
    mov rdi, 1         ; fd for stdout = 1
    mov rsi, buffer    ; const void *buf (our read buffer)
    syscall            ; rax = bytes written or negative error code
    ; Basic error check omitted for sys_write brevity
    jmp read_loop      ; Go back and try to read more data

read_done:
    ; Check if read returned an error (rax < 0)
    cmp rax, 0
    jl read_error      ; Jump if read error occurred

    ; --- Close file (sys_close) ---
close_file: ; Label used by error handlers too
    mov rax, 3         ; syscall number for sys_close
    mov rdi, [fd]      ; int fd
    syscall            ; rax = 0 on success, -1 on error
    ; Error check omitted for close

    ; --- Exit program normally (sys_exit) ---
    mov rax, 60        ; syscall number for sys_exit
    mov rdi, 0         ; exit code 0 (success)
    syscall

; --- Error Handling Sections ---
open_error:
    mov rax, 1         ; sys_write
    mov rdi, 2         ; fd for stderr = 2
    mov rsi, errmsg_open
    mov rdx, len_errmsg_open
    syscall
    mov rax, 60        ; sys_exit
    mov rdi, 1         ; exit code 1 (error)
    syscall

read_error:
    mov rax, 1         ; sys_write
    mov rdi, 2         ; fd for stderr = 2
    mov rsi, errmsg_read
    mov rdx, len_errmsg_read
    syscall
    jmp close_file     ; Try to close the file even after read error before exiting

; To assemble and run:
; nasm -f elf64 snippet.asm -o snippet.o
; ld snippet.o -o snippet
; echo "Hello from example.txt file." > example.txt
; ./snippet
writeFile:
; NASM syntax (Linux 64-bit using syscalls)
section .data
    filename db "output.txt", 0 ; File to write to
    contentToWrite db "Hello, this is line 1 written from Assembly.", 10, "This is line 2.", 10
    contentLen equ $ - contentToWrite ; Calculate length at compile time
    errmsg_open db "Error opening file for writing", 10
    len_errmsg_open equ $ - errmsg_open
    errmsg_write db "Error writing to file", 10
    len_errmsg_write equ $ - errmsg_write

section .bss
    fd resq 1                    ; Reserve space for file descriptor (64-bit)

section .text
    global _start

_start:
    ; --- Open file for writing (sys_open) ---
    ; Flags: O_WRONLY | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC
    ; O_WRONLY = 1
    ; O_CREAT  = 64 (0o100)
    ; O_TRUNC  = 512 (0o1000)
    ; Total flags = 1 + 64 + 512 = 577 (0o1101)
    ; Mode: 0644 (rw-r--r--) = 420 decimal (0o644)
    mov rax, 2         ; syscall number for sys_open
    mov rdi, filename  ; const char *pathname
    mov rsi, 0o1101    ; flags (O_WRONLY | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC) in octal
    mov rdx, 0o644     ; mode (octal) for file permissions
    syscall            ; rax = file descriptor or negative error code

    cmp rax, 0         ; Check if rax is negative (error)
    jl open_error

    mov [fd], rax      ; Store the file descriptor

    ; --- Write content to file (sys_write) ---
    mov rax, 1         ; syscall number for sys_write
    mov rdi, [fd]      ; int fd (the one we just opened)
    mov rsi, contentToWrite ; const void *buf (our data)
    mov rdx, contentLen ; size_t count (length of our data)
    syscall            ; rax = bytes written or negative error code

    cmp rax, 0         ; Check for error (rax < 0 indicates error)
    jl write_error
    cmp rax, contentLen ; Check if all expected bytes were written
    jne write_error    ; Partial write is also treated as an error here for simplicity

    ; --- Close file (sys_close) ---
close_file: ; Label used by error handlers too
    mov rax, 3         ; syscall number for sys_close
    mov rdi, [fd]      ; int fd
    syscall            ; rax = 0 on success, -1 on error
    ; Error check omitted for close

    ; --- Exit program normally (sys_exit) ---
    mov rax, 60        ; syscall number for sys_exit
    mov rdi, 0         ; exit code 0 (success)
    syscall

; --- Error Handling Sections ---
open_error:
    mov rax, 1         ; sys_write
    mov rdi, 2         ; fd for stderr = 2
    mov rsi, errmsg_open
    mov rdx, len_errmsg_open
    syscall
    mov rax, 60        ; sys_exit
    mov rdi, 1         ; exit code 1 (error)
    syscall

write_error:
    mov rax, 1         ; sys_write
    mov rdi, 2         ; fd for stderr = 2
    mov rsi, errmsg_write
    mov rdx, len_errmsg_write
    syscall
    jmp close_file     ; Try to close the file even after write error before exiting

; To assemble and run:
; nasm -f elf64 snippet.asm -o snippet.o
; ld snippet.o -o snippet
; ./snippet
; cat output.txt  ; Verify the content was written
fileExists:
; NASM syntax (Linux 64-bit using sys_access syscall)
section .data
    existingFile db "code-snippets.php", 0 ; Assume this file exists
    nonExistingFile db "nonexistent_xyz123.txt", 0
    msg_exists db "'%s' exists.", 10, 0
    msg_not_exists db "'%s' does NOT exist.", 10, 0
    ; Using printf for output requires linking C library

section .text
    extern printf ; Using printf for simpler output formatting
    global main   ; Use main entry point for linking with libc

main:
    push rbp
    mov rbp, rsp
    sub rsp, 8 ; Align stack

    ; --- Check existing file ---
    mov rdi, existingFile
    call check_file_exists ; Call our helper function
    mov rdx, rax           ; Save result (0 or 1) in rdx
    mov rdi, msg_exists    ; Assume exists first for printf format string
    cmp rdx, 0             ; Compare result with 0
    je .no1                ; If 0 (doesn't exist), jump to use other message
.yes1:
    mov rsi, existingFile  ; Second arg for printf: filename
    mov eax, 0             ; No vector args for printf
    call printf
    jmp .check_next        ; Jump to check the next file
.no1:
    mov rdi, msg_not_exists ; Use the "does not exist" message
    mov rsi, existingFile
    mov eax, 0
    call printf

.check_next:
    ; --- Check non-existing file ---
    mov rdi, nonExistingFile
    call check_file_exists
    mov rdx, rax
    mov rdi, msg_exists
    cmp rdx, 0
    je .no2
.yes2:
    mov rsi, nonExistingFile
    mov eax, 0
    call printf
    jmp .done
.no2:
    mov rdi, msg_not_exists
    mov rsi, nonExistingFile
    mov eax, 0
    call printf

.done:
    ; --- Exit program ---
    mov rsp, rbp
    pop rbp
    mov eax, 0      ; Return 0 from main
    ret


; Function: check_file_exists
; Checks if a file exists using Linux sys_access syscall.
; Input: RDI = pointer to null-terminated filename string
; Output: RAX = 1 if file exists and is accessible, 0 otherwise
check_file_exists:
    push rbp
    mov rbp, rsp

    ; Use sys_access syscall to check for file existence
    mov rax, 21         ; syscall number for sys_access (on x86_64)
                        ; Note: On 32-bit Linux it's 33.
    ; RDI already contains the filename pointer (first argument)
    mov rsi, 0          ; mode (F_OK = 0, just test for existence)
    syscall             ; rax = 0 on success (file exists), -errno on error

    ; Check the result from syscall
    test rax, rax       ; Sets flags based on rax. Checks if rax is 0.
    mov rax, 0          ; Assume doesn't exist (return 0)
    jz .fileExists      ; If syscall result was 0 (ZF=1), it exists, jump
    jmp .doneCheck      ; Otherwise, keep rax=0 and return
.fileExists:
    mov rax, 1          ; Set return value to 1 (exists)
.doneCheck:
    mov rsp, rbp
    pop rbp
    ret

; To assemble and link (requires GCC/Clang):
; nasm -f elf64 snippet.asm -o snippet.o
; gcc snippet.o -o snippet -no-pie
; touch code-snippets.php   ; Create one file to test existence
; ./snippet                 ; Run the executable
dateTime:
; NASM syntax (Linux 64-bit syscall sys_time)
; Gets seconds since epoch. Formatting requires more complex code or libc.
section .data
    msg db "Seconds since epoch: %ld", 10, 0
    errmsg db "Error getting time", 10
    len_errmsg equ $ - errmsg
    ; Using printf requires linking C library

section .bss
    epoch_time resq 1    ; Reserve 8 bytes for time_t result (optional storage)

section .text
    extern printf
    global main ; Use main for linking with libc

main:
    push rbp
    mov rbp, rsp
    sub rsp, 8 ; Align stack

    ; --- Get current time using sys_time ---
    ; syscall sys_time(time_t *tloc);
    ; Returns time in RAX, and optionally stores it at address in RDI.
    mov rax, 201       ; syscall number for sys_time (on x86_64)
                       ; Note: On 32-bit it's 13.
    ; mov rdi, epoch_time ; Optionally store result here too
    mov rdi, 0          ; Pass NULL, time will just be returned in RAX
    syscall             ; rax = current time (seconds since epoch), or negative error code

    cmp rax, 0
    jl time_error      ; Handle error if rax < 0

    ; --- Print the epoch time using printf ---
    ; RAX already contains the epoch time value
    mov rdi, msg       ; Format string
    mov rsi, rax       ; The epoch time value (passed as long)
    mov eax, 0         ; No vector registers used for printf
    call printf

    ; --- Exit program normally ---
exit_program:
    mov rsp, rbp
    pop rbp
    mov eax, 0      ; Return 0 from main
    ret

time_error:
    ; Simple error handling: print message to stderr and exit with error code
    mov rax, 1         ; sys_write
    mov rdi, 2         ; fd for stderr = 2
    mov rsi, errmsg
    mov rdx, len_errmsg
    syscall
    mov rax, 60        ; sys_exit
    mov rdi, 1         ; exit code 1
    syscall

; Note: Formatting this raw epoch time into a human-readable date/time string
; (like YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS) in pure assembly is very complex. It usually
; involves either implementing calendar algorithms or calling C library
; functions like localtime() and strftime(). This snippet only gets the raw timestamp.

; To assemble and link (requires GCC/Clang):
; nasm -f elf64 snippet.asm -o snippet.o
; gcc snippet.o -o snippet -no-pie
; ./snippet
maxInArray:
; NASM syntax (Linux 64-bit, finds max in a predefined array)
; Uses printf for output, requires linking C library.
section .data
    numbers dq 10, 5, 42, 17, 8, 99, 32 ; Define array of 64-bit integers (dq)
    array_size equ ($ - numbers) / 8     ; Calculate number of elements (8 bytes per dq)

    msg_array db "The array is:", 0
    msg_max db "Maximum value: %ld", 10, 0
    fmt_num db " %ld", 0 ; Format for printing numbers in array
    newline db 10, 0
    msg_empty db "Array is empty, cannot find maximum.", 10, 0

section .text
    extern printf
    global main

main:
    push rbp
    mov rbp, rsp
    sub rsp, 8 ; Align stack

    ; --- Check if array is empty ---
    cmp array_size, 0
    jle empty_array       ; Handle empty array case (size <= 0)

    ; --- Print the array elements (Optional, for context) ---
    mov rdi, msg_array
    mov eax, 0
    call printf

    mov rcx, 0           ; Loop counter i = 0
    mov rbx, numbers     ; Pointer to the start of the array
print_loop:
    cmp rcx, array_size  ; Compare i with size
    jge print_done       ; If i >= size, exit loop

    mov rdi, fmt_num     ; Format string for number
    mov rsi, [rbx + rcx*8] ; Load array element arr[i] (scale index by 8 bytes for qword)
    mov eax, 0
    call printf

    inc rcx              ; i
	inc rcx              ; i++
    jmp print_loop       ; Continue loop

print_done:
    mov rdi, newline     ; Print newline after array
    mov eax, 0
    call printf

    ; --- Find maximum value in array ---
    mov rax, [numbers]   ; Initialize max with first element arr[0]
    mov rcx, 1           ; Start loop counter from 1 (since we already have arr[0])
    mov rbx, numbers     ; Pointer to the start of the array

max_loop:
    cmp rcx, array_size  ; Compare i with size
    jge max_done         ; If i >= size, exit loop

    mov rdx, [rbx + rcx*8] ; Load array element arr[i] (scale index by 8 bytes for qword)
    cmp rdx, rax         ; Compare arr[i] with current max
    jle skip_update      ; If arr[i] <= max, skip update
    mov rax, rdx         ; Update max = arr[i]

skip_update:
    inc rcx              ; i++
    jmp max_loop         ; Continue loop

max_done:
    ; --- Print the maximum value ---
    mov rdi, msg_max     ; Format string
    mov rsi, rax         ; The maximum value
    mov eax, 0           ; No vector registers used for printf
    call printf

    ; --- Exit program normally ---
    mov rsp, rbp
    pop rbp
    mov eax, 0           ; Return 0 from main
    ret

empty_array:
    mov rdi, msg_empty
    mov eax, 0
    call printf
    jmp exit_program

exit_program:
    mov rsp, rbp
    pop rbp
    mov eax, 0
    ret

; nasm -f elf64 snippet.asm -o snippet.o
; gcc snippet.o -o snippet -no-pie
